metadata.summary: Design and configure GCP resource hierarchy (org/folders/projects), naming conventions, required labels, and project setup to enforce governance at scale.

content.title: GCP Resource Hierarchy Design
content.summary: GCP organizes all resources in a 4-level tree: Organization → Folder → Project → Resource. IAM policies and organization constraints flow downward; projects are the billing boundary and API-enablement boundary. Designing this hierarchy upfront prevents costly restructuring later.

why.p: The resource hierarchy controls IAM inheritance, cost attribution, and policy enforcement. A well-designed hierarchy lets you apply a security constraint once at the organization level and have it propagate to every project automatically. Poorly designed hierarchies (e.g., mixing environments inside one project, or using more than 3 folder levels) create policy complexity that becomes intractable at scale.

list.item: Setting up new GCP organizations or migrating standalone projects into an organization structure.
Designing folder and project layouts for new teams, environments, or applications.
Planning multi-environment deployments (dev/staging/prod) with clear separation boundaries.
Establishing governance and security foundations before teams start creating resources.
Single personal project with no organizational requirements — flat project structure is sufficient.
Read-only GCP audit tasks — hierarchy restructuring requires Organization Admin and should be done in a planned change window.

section.name: hierarchy-levels
section.p: The 4 levels of the GCP resource hierarchy and their roles:

list.item: Organization (root) — company-wide policies, billing account linkage, break-glass admin access. Policies flow down to all children.
Folder (department/team/env) — groups projects by team, environment, or application. Inherits org policies, can add its own.
Project (workload boundary) — billing boundary and API-enablement boundary. Inherits folder policies.
Resource (VMs, buckets, etc.) — actual cloud services. Inherit project policies.

rule.statement: Keep folder nesting depth at 3 or fewer levels. Deeply nested hierarchies increase policy complexity without adding governance value.
rule.rationale: Each additional folder level adds an inheritance layer to debug when IAM or org-policy issues occur. Flat hierarchies are easier to reason about and audit.

rule.statement: Separate environments (dev/staging/prod) into different projects, not different folders inside one project. Each environment must have its own project.
rule.rationale: Projects are the billing and API-enablement boundary. Mixing environments in one project makes it impossible to restrict prod APIs from dev workloads or attribute costs separately.

section.name: folder-structure-patterns
section.p: Three proven patterns for folder structure:

example.title: Enterprise organization structure
example.code: acme-corp (Organization) ├── bootstrap/ │ ├── prj-b-seed # Terraform state, automation │ └── prj-b-cicd # CI/CD pipelines │ ├── common/ │ ├── prj-c-logging # Centralized logging │ ├── prj-c-monitoring # Shared monitoring │ ├── prj-c-networking-hub # Hub VPC, interconnect │ └── prj-c-security # Security tools │ ├── production/ │ ├── prj-p-app1 # Production app 1 │ ├── prj-p-app2 # Production app 2 │ └── prj-p-data # Production data platform │ ├── staging/ │ ├── prj-s-app1 # Staging app 1 │ └── prj-s-data # Staging data platform │ └── development/ ├── prj-d-app1 # Development app 1 └── prj-d-sandbox # Developer sandbox
example.note: Bootstrap and common folders hold shared infrastructure. Environment separation (production/staging/development) is at the folder level, not the project level.

example.title: Startup organization structure
example.code: startup-io (Organization) ├── shared/ │ └── prj-shared-infra # Shared networking, logging │ ├── prod/ │ └── prj-prod-main # Production workloads │ └── nonprod/ ├── prj-staging # Staging environment └── prj-dev # Development environment
example.note: Minimal 2-level folder depth. Shared services folder for common resources keeps the hierarchy flat.

example.title: Multi-team organization structure
example.code: company-xyz (Organization) ├── platform/ │ ├── prj-platform-prod # Platform team prod │ └── prj-platform-dev # Platform team dev │ ├── team-alpha/ │ ├── prj-alpha-prod # Team Alpha prod │ └── prj-alpha-dev # Team Alpha dev │ └── team-beta/ ├── prj-beta-prod # Team Beta prod └── prj-beta-dev # Team Beta dev
example.note: Folder per team enables delegated folder admin without granting org-level access to team leads.

section.name: naming-and-labels
section.p: Consistent naming and mandatory labels are the foundation of cost tracking and operational visibility.

rule.statement: All projects MUST follow the naming pattern: {resource-type}-{environment}-{application}. Project IDs must be globally unique and immutable once created — include a short hex suffix if needed.
rule.rationale: Project names are mutable but project IDs are not. Using a deterministic prefix scheme (prj-prod-ecommerce) makes the hierarchy navigable without console access and avoids naming conflicts.

rule.statement: Projects MUST carry at minimum three labels: env (prod|staging|dev), team (owning team), cost_center (cost center code). Enforce this via an org policy custom constraint.
rule.rationale: Labels are the only mechanism for attributing costs to teams and cost centers in BigQuery billing exports. Projects without labels appear as unattributed spend in cost reports.

example.title: Standard naming pattern
example.code:
{resource-type}-{environment}-{application}-{component}
Examples: - prj-prod-ecommerce # Project - vpc-prod-ecommerce # VPC - gke-prod-ecommerce-primary # GKE cluster - sql-prod-ecommerce-main # Cloud SQL - sa-prod-ecommerce-api # Service account - bkt-prod-ecommerce-assets # Storage bucket

example.title: Required label standards
example.code:
Required Labels: env: prod|staging|dev team: platform|backend|frontend|data cost_center: cc-NNN managed_by: terraform|manual
Optional Labels: app: application-name component: api|web|worker owner: team-email

antipattern.description: Deeply nested folder hierarchies (4+ levels) to reflect every organizational sub-unit.
antipattern.reason: Policy inheritance becomes difficult to trace and audit. Each extra level adds one more layer to check when debugging IAM denials or org-policy violations.

antipattern.description: No resource labels on projects, VMs, or buckets.
antipattern.reason: Labels are the only mechanism for cost attribution in BigQuery billing exports and Looker Studio dashboards. Unlabeled resources appear as unattributed spend.

antipattern.description: Mixing dev and prod workloads inside the same project.
antipattern.reason: Projects are the API-enablement and billing boundary. Mixed-environment projects make cost attribution impossible and increase the blast radius of accidental changes.

section.name: project-setup
section.p: Each new project must be set up with a consistent checklist:

checklist.check: Standardized naming convention applied; project ID follows naming standards.
Required labels attached (env, team, cost-center, managed_by).
Only necessary APIs enabled — review with gcloud services list --enabled.
Default service account reviewed and disabled if not required.
Project linked to correct billing account.
auto_create_network set to false in Terraform (no default VPC).

example.title: Create project with labels via gcloud
example.code:
# Create project in folder gcloud projects create prj-prod-app1 \ --folder=FOLDER_ID \ --name="Production App 1" \ --labels="env=prod,team=platform,cost-center=cc-001"
# Link billing account gcloud billing projects link prj-prod-app1 \ --billing-account=BILLING_ACCOUNT_ID
# Enable required APIs gcloud services enable \ compute.googleapis.com \ container.googleapis.com \ cloudsql.googleapis.com \ secretmanager.googleapis.com \ --project=prj-prod-app1

example.title: Terraform project factory module

checklist.check: Organization resource created (via Cloud Identity or Workspace).
Organization Admin role assigned to designated admins (break-glass only).
Organization policies configured (constraints) — resource locations, SA key creation.
Audit logging enabled at org level.
Shared services folder for common resources (logging, monitoring, networking-hub).
Bootstrap folder for IaC and automation (Terraform seed project).
Hierarchy kept flat — minimize nesting depth to 3 or fewer.

section.name: llm-prompts
section.prompt:
Design a GCP resource hierarchy for [ORGANIZATION DESCRIPTION]:
Organization Context: - Company size: [startup/SMB/enterprise] - Teams: [list teams/departments] - Environments: [dev/staging/prod/sandbox] - Compliance requirements: [GDPR/HIPAA/SOC2/none] - Multi-region: [yes/no, which regions]
Requirements: 1. Design folder structure reflecting organizational boundaries 2. Propose naming conventions for projects 3. Identify shared services projects needed 4. Define IAM inheritance strategy 5. Recommend organization policies
Deliverables: - Visual hierarchy diagram (text-based) - Folder and project naming conventions - IAM role recommendations per level - Organization policies to enforce

Help migrate from standalone GCP projects to organization structure:
Current State: - Projects: [list existing projects] - Billing accounts: [number and purpose] - Current IAM: [describe current access model]
Target State: - Organization domain: [domain.com] - Desired folder structure: [describe] - Environment separation: [requirements]
Requirements: 1. Migration path from standalone to org 2. Project migration order and dependencies 3. IAM migration strategy 4. Billing account consolidation 5. Minimal disruption approach

Audit GCP resource hierarchy for [ORGANIZATION]:
Audit Areas: 1. Folder depth and complexity 2. Naming consistency 3. IAM policy inheritance issues 4. Organization policy gaps 5. Orphaned projects 6. Billing account assignments
Output: - Current state assessment - Issues by severity - Remediation recommendations - Migration plan if restructuring needed

Design project structure for [APPLICATION/PLATFORM]:
Application Context: - Type: [web app/data platform/ML/microservices] - Components: [list major components] - Data sensitivity: [public/internal/confidential] - Traffic patterns: [describe load]
Environment Requirements: - Development: [requirements] - Staging: [requirements] - Production: [requirements]
Design Requirements: 1. Project per environment vs shared 2. API enablement per project 3. Network connectivity between projects 4. Shared resources strategy 5. Cost attribution approach
